mission.title: linkerd: Support RSA certificates from external PKI
mission.description: Support RSA certificates from external PKI. Requested by 5+ users.

steps.title: Check current linkerd deployment
steps.description:
Verify your linkerd version and configuration:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n linkerd -l app.kubernetes.io/name=linkerd
helm list -n linkerd 2>/dev/null || echo "Not installed via Helm"
```
This feature requires a working linkerd installation.

steps.title: Review linkerd configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the relevant linkerd configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n linkerd -l app.kubernetes.io/name=linkerd
kubectl get configmap -n linkerd -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=linkerd
```
## Feature Request
We would like to onboard to using an external CA, but unfortunately they do not support ECDSA, only RSA:
```
Error: failed to read CA from linkerd-identity-issuer: must use ECDSA for public key algorithm, instead RSA was

steps.title: Apply the fix for Support RSA certificates from external PKI
steps.description:
Sorry, I mean the Key and CSR. The question is can the ECDSA CSR be signed by the RSA issuer key? If that works, I think we could relax some of the service-side checks. We'd need to validate that the proxies can still verify the certs, obviously; and it would be helpful to understand how the
```yaml
Error: failed to read CA from linkerd-identity-issuer: must use ECDSA for public key algorithm, instead RSA was used
```

steps.title: Verify the feature works
steps.description:
Test that the new capability is working as expected:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n linkerd -l app.kubernetes.io/name=linkerd
kubectl get events -n linkerd --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm the feature described in "Support RSA certificates from external PKI" is functioning correctly.

resolution.summary: Sorry, I mean the Key and CSR. The question is can the ECDSA CSR be signed by the RSA issuer key? If that works, I think we could relax some of the service-side checks. We'd need to validate that the proxies can still verify the certs, obviously; and it would be helpful to understand how the resulting certs differ (especially in terms of size).
